content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamainternalmedicine/fullarticle/2771095", "title": "Effects of Time-Restricted Eating on Weight Loss and Other Metabolic Parameters (TREAT randomized clinical trial)", "summary": "Lowe et al. JAMA Internal Medicine 2020 RCT (NCT03393195, UCSF/UHCC). 141 adults with overweight or obesity randomized to 16:8 time-restricted eating (TRE; eat 12:00pm-8:00pm) or consistent meal timing (CMT; 3 meals/day) for 12 weeks; 105 completed protocol. Primary outcome weight change: TRE -0.94 kg (95% CI -1.68 to -0.20, P=.01), CMT -0.68 kg (95% CI -1.41 to 0.05, P=.07), between-group difference -0.26 kg (95% CI -1.30 to 0.78, P=.63 — NOT statistically significant). Self-reported adherence: CMT 92.1%, TRE 83.5%. Critical safety signal: TRE produced significant loss of appendicular lean mass (-0.64 kg, 95% CI -0.89 to -0.39, P<.001) with a significant between-group difference (-0.47 kg, P=.009) favoring CMT (less lean loss). No within- or between-group differences in fasting glucose, fasting insulin, HOMA-IR, HbA1C, triglycerides, total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, or blood pressure. Methodology: ITT analysis via linear mixed model, 3-knot cubic splines, Winsorized sensitivity analysis, DXA body composition in 46-participant in-person cohort. Confidence: HIGH for methodology; HIGH for negative cardiometabolic findings; HIGH for lean-mass concern. Contradiction with common IF claims: TRE does not produce meaningful weight advantage over consistent meal timing and raises safety concern about appendicular lean mass.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Methodological", "Public_Health"], "key_facts": ["141 adults randomized, 105 completed 12 weeks (74.5% completion)", "TRE vs CMT weight diff: -0.26 kg (95% CI -1.30 to 0.78, P=.63) — NOT significant", "TRE lost appendicular lean mass -0.64 kg (95% CI -0.89 to -0.39, P<.001)", "Between-group appendicular lean mass diff: -0.47 kg (P=.009) favoring CMT", "No significant between-group differences in glucose, insulin, HbA1C, lipids, or BP"], "knowledge_contribution": "Primary-data evidence that 16:8 TRE produces no clinically meaningful weight loss advantage over structured 3-meal timing AND causes significant appendicular lean mass loss relative to control. Contradicts many popular IF claims; supports cautious interpretation of TRE."}, {"url": "https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34135111/", "title": "A randomized controlled trial to isolate the effects of fasting and energy restriction on weight loss and metabolic health in lean adults (Templeman et al., Science Translational Medicine, 2021)", "summary": "Templeman et al. Sci Transl Med 2021 (PMID 34135111, DOI 10.1126/scitranslmed.abd8034). 3-arm factorial RCT in lean healthy adults (n=36 total, 12 per arm) for 3 weeks, designed to isolate fasting effects from energy restriction effects. Arms: (1) 75:75 = continuous 75% energy intake every day (daily energy restriction, no fasting); (2) 0:150 = alternate-day fasting with 150% energy intake on feed days (ADF matched to 75% average energy intake); (3) 0:200 = alternate-day fasting with 200% energy intake on feed days (fasting without net energy restriction). Body mass change: 75:75 -1.91 ± 0.99 kg (fat -1.75 ± 0.79 kg); 0:150 -1.60 ± 1.06 kg (fat only -0.74 ± 1.32 kg, P=0.01 vs 75:75); 0:200 -0.52 ± 1.09 kg body mass, -0.12 ± 0.68 kg fat. Postprandial cardiometabolic indices, gut hormones, and subcutaneous adipose gene expression did not differ between groups (P > 0.05). Authors' conclusion: 'alternate-day fasting less effectively reduces body fat mass than a matched degree of daily energy restriction and without evidence of fasting-specific effects on metabolic regulation or cardiovascular health.' Confidence: HIGH for within-study findings (isolation design is rigorous); MEDIUM for generalizability (lean adults, 3 weeks, n=36). Contradiction with common IF claims: ADF does NOT carry fasting-specific metabolic benefits once energy balance is matched.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["3-arm RCT, n=36 lean adults (12 per arm), 3-week duration, Templeman 2021", "75:75 daily ER: body mass -1.91 ± 0.99 kg, fat -1.75 ± 0.79 kg", "0:150 ADF matched ER: body mass -1.60 ± 1.06 kg but fat only -0.74 ± 1.32 kg (P=0.01 vs 75:75)", "0:200 ADF without ER: body mass -0.52 ± 1.09 kg, fat -0.12 ± 0.68 kg", "No between-group differences in postprandial cardiometabolic indices, gut hormones, or adipose gene expression (P>0.05)"], "knowledge_contribution": "Mechanistic-isolation RCT showing fasting PER SE confers no metabolic benefit beyond energy restriction; ADF is inferior to daily ER for fat loss when energy intake is matched. Strengthens case that intermittent fasting benefits observed elsewhere are explained by caloric restriction, not fasting-specific mechanisms."}]}
